segments.title: HMS Hermes cruiser-carrier scenario starts from a patron prompt
segments.summary: The video frames a counterfactual around Britain building ten small Hermes-style carriers between 1926 and 1930.
segments.body: The prompt is treated as a scenario that needs a historically viable path.

evidence.note: Opening reads the small aircraft carrier prompt and states that a viable historical scenario exists.

segments.title: Washington Treaty small-carrier loopholes
segments.summary: The treaty section explains how carrier tonnage and under-10,000-ton ships shape the Hermes scenario.
segments.body: This is the legal/treaty basis for the cruiser-carrier counterfactual.

evidence.note: The treaty section explains how carrier tonnage and under-10,000-ton ships shape the Hermes scenario.

segments.title: Britain needs carriers in multiple theatres
segments.summary: The episode lists Far East, Indian Ocean, Mediterranean, North Atlantic, and South Atlantic carrier needs.
segments.body: The chapter lists the number of places Britain might want carrier aviation: the Far East, Indian Ocean, Mediterranean, North Atlantic, and South Atlantic. Dr. Clarke uses that geographic spread to explain why numerous smaller carriers can look attractive even when each ship is compromised. The issue is not only deck size, but whether the navy can have aviation present where crises occur.

evidence.note: The episode lists Far East, Indian Ocean, Mediterranean, North Atlantic, and South Atlantic carrier needs.

segments.title: Hermes design constraints and small carrier compromises
segments.summary: The discussion turns to Hermes herself and the compromises of making small carriers work.
segments.body: The Hermes section keeps the small-carrier counterfactual tied to actual ship limits. Dr. Clarke turns from treaty arithmetic to the compromises of making Hermes-like carriers operate aircraft, survive at sea, and fit into cruiser-style deployment patterns. That prevents the scenario from treating every sub-10,000-ton carrier as equally practical.

evidence.note: The discussion turns to Hermes herself and the compromises of making small carriers work.

segments.title: Cruiser carriers distributed around stations
segments.summary: The episode develops a model where cruiser carriers accompany cruisers and provide forward aviation presence.
segments.body: The deployment model imagines cruiser carriers distributed across stations rather than concentrated only with the battle fleet. Dr. Clarke uses the concept to show how small carriers could give cruisers local scouting and aviation support while extending imperial presence. The operational value comes from coverage and availability, not from pretending these ships equal full fleet carriers.

evidence.note: The episode develops a model where cruiser carriers accompany cruisers and provide forward aviation presence.

segments.title: Other navies react to the small carrier category
segments.summary: The middle-late section explores how other navies might respond to British small carriers.
segments.body: The middle-late section asks how other navies would react if Britain normalized a useful small-carrier category. Dr. Clarke broadens the scenario beyond the Royal Navy by considering whether rivals and partners would imitate, counter, or exploit the same treaty space. That reaction matters because a British design choice can change the wider naval architecture of the period.

evidence.note: The middle-late section explores how other navies might respond to British small carriers.

segments.title: A light carrier treaty category emerges
segments.summary: The scenario suggests a light carrier category around 13,500 tons.
segments.body: The light-carrier section turns the Hermes alternative into a treaty-system consequence. Dr. Clarke suggests a category around 13,500 tons, showing how a successful small carrier could reshape what naval powers asked the treaty system to recognize. The point is that design practice and treaty language can evolve together.

evidence.note: The scenario suggests a light carrier category around 13,500 tons.

segments.title: Forward British carrier presence expands
segments.summary: The later section argues smaller carriers could keep a British or imperial carrier presence forward more often.
segments.body: The forward-presence section explains why a larger number of smaller carriers could matter even if each ship is individually limited. Dr. Clarke argues that British or imperial carrier aviation could appear in more places more often, especially alongside cruiser forces. The strategic payoff is persistent presence and scouting reach rather than a single decisive fleet-air-arm concentration.

evidence.note: The later section argues smaller carriers could keep a British or imperial carrier presence forward more often.

segments.title: North Atlantic effect on Deutschland
segments.summary: The conclusion asks whether extra small carriers in the North Atlantic affect Deutschland making it home.
segments.body: The Deutschland question turns the cruiser-carrier scenario into a wartime consequence. Dr. Clarke asks whether additional small carriers in the North Atlantic might change the odds of the German raider getting home. The value of the segment is that it tests the counterfactual against an identifiable operation rather than leaving it at fleet-design theory.

evidence.note: The conclusion asks whether extra small carriers in the North Atlantic affect Deutschland making it home.
